From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

|  |  |
| --- | --- |
|  | This article needs to be **updated**. The reason given is: **The article does not reflect the changes introduced by the 2024 Law about [populated places in Ukraine](/wiki/Populated_places_in_Ukraine "Populated places in Ukraine")**. Please help update this article to reflect recent events or newly available information. *(May 2025)* |

Third-level administrative divisions of Ukraine

This article is about the administrative division in Ukraine. For the use in Poland, see [Gromada](/wiki/Gromada "Gromada"). For the use in Belarus, see [Hramada](/wiki/Hramada "Hramada"). For the former division, see [Amalgamated hromada](/wiki/Amalgamated_hromada "Amalgamated hromada"). For other uses, see [Hromada (disambiguation)](/wiki/Hromada_(disambiguation) "Hromada (disambiguation)").

|  |
| --- |
| Part of [a series](/wiki/Category:Administrative_divisions_of_Ukraine "Category:Administrative divisions of Ukraine") on the |
| [Subdivisions of Ukraine](/wiki/Administrative_divisions_of_Ukraine "Administrative divisions of Ukraine") |
| [Map of the subdivisions of Ukraine](/wiki/File:Map_of_Ukraine_Oblasts_simple_4_colors.svg "Map of the subdivisions of Ukraine") |
| First level |
| * [1 autonomous republic](/wiki/Autonomous_republic_of_Ukraine "Autonomous republic of Ukraine") * [24 oblasts](/wiki/Oblasts_of_Ukraine "Oblasts of Ukraine") * [2 cities with special status](/wiki/City_with_special_status "City with special status") |
| Second level |
| * [136 raions](/wiki/Raions_of_Ukraine "Raions of Ukraine") |
| Third level |
| * 1469 hromadas |
| This sidebar:   * [view](/wiki/Template:Ukraine_subdivisions_sidebar "Template:Ukraine subdivisions sidebar") * [talk](/wiki/Template_talk:Ukraine_subdivisions_sidebar "Template talk:Ukraine subdivisions sidebar") * [edit](/wiki/Special:EditPage/Template:Ukraine_subdivisions_sidebar "Special:EditPage/Template:Ukraine subdivisions sidebar") |

In [Ukraine](/wiki/Ukraine "Ukraine"), a **hromada** (громада [[ɦrɔˈmadɐ]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Ukrainian "Help:IPA/Ukrainian"); lit. 'community') is the main type of [municipality](/wiki/Municipality "Municipality") and the third level [local self-government in Ukraine](/wiki/Administrative_divisions_of_Ukraine "Administrative divisions of Ukraine"). The current hromadas were established by the [Government of Ukraine](/wiki/Cabinet_of_ministers_of_Ukraine "Cabinet of ministers of Ukraine") on 12 June 2020.[[1]](#cite_note-:0-1)

A municipality is designated *urban hromada* if its administration is located in a [city](/wiki/City "City"); *settlement hromada* if it is located in a settlement (*selyshche*), and *rural hromada* if it is located in a village (*[selo](/wiki/Village_(Ukraine) "Village (Ukraine)")*) or a *[selyshche](/wiki/Rural_settlement_(Ukraine) "Rural settlement (Ukraine)")*.[[2]](#cite_note-2) Hromadas are grouped to form [raions](/wiki/Raions_of_Ukraine "Raions of Ukraine") (districts); groups of raions form [oblasts](/wiki/Oblasts_of_Ukraine "Oblasts of Ukraine") (regions). Optionally, a municipality may be divided into [starosta okruhs](/wiki/Starosta_okruh "Starosta okruh") (similar to [civil parishes](/wiki/Civil_parish "Civil parish") in Great Britain or [frazioni](/wiki/Frazione "Frazione") in Italy), which are the lowest level of local government in Ukraine.

Similar terms exist in [Poland](/wiki/Poland "Poland") (*[gromada](/wiki/Gromada "Gromada")*) and in [Belarus](/wiki/Belarus "Belarus") (*[hramada](/wiki/Hramada "Hramada")*). The literal translation of this term is "[community](/wiki/Community "Community")", similar to the terms used in western European states, such as Germany (*[Gemeinde](/wiki/Gemeinde_(Germany) "Gemeinde (Germany)")*), France (*[commune](/wiki/Commune_(France) "Commune (France)")*), Italy (*[comune](/wiki/Comune "Comune")*), and Portugal (*[freguesia](/wiki/Freguesia "Freguesia")*), or in several English-speaking countries (*[township](/wiki/Township "Township")*).

## List of hromadas

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Hromada&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: List of hromadas")]

Main article: [List of hromadas of Ukraine](/wiki/List_of_hromadas_of_Ukraine "List of hromadas of Ukraine")

In total, there are 1469 hromadas (as of 1 October 2023), including:

* 409 *urban hromadas* ([Ukrainian](/wiki/Ukrainian_language "Ukrainian language"): міська громада),
* 435 *selyshche hromadas* ([Ukrainian](/wiki/Ukrainian_language "Ukrainian language"): селищна громада),
* 625 *rural hromadas* ([Ukrainian](/wiki/Ukrainian_language "Ukrainian language"): сільська громада).

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Hromada&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: History")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/03/%D0%9E%D0%A2%D0%932.png/250px-%D0%9E%D0%A2%D0%932.png)](/wiki/File:%D0%9E%D0%A2%D0%932.png)

[Hromadas of Ukraine](/wiki/List_of_hromadas_of_Ukraine "List of hromadas of Ukraine") as of 2020 ([raions](/wiki/Raion "Raion") in [Crimea](/wiki/Crimea "Crimea"))

Prior to 2020, the basic units of administrative division in Ukraine were rural councils, settlement councils and city councils, which were often referred to by the generic term *hromada*.

The [Constitution of Ukraine](/wiki/Constitution_of_Ukraine "Constitution of Ukraine") and some other laws, including the "Law on local self-governance", delegate certain rights and obligations for hromadas. Types of hromadas include cities, [rural settlements](/wiki/Rural_settlement_(Ukraine) "Rural settlement (Ukraine)"), and villages. In his draft constitutional amendments of June 2014, [President](/wiki/President_of_Ukraine "President of Ukraine") [Petro Poroshenko](/wiki/Petro_Poroshenko "Petro Poroshenko") proposed changing the [administrative divisions of Ukraine](/wiki/Administrative_divisions_of_Ukraine "Administrative divisions of Ukraine"), which he felt should include [oblasts](/wiki/Oblasts_of_Ukraine "Oblasts of Ukraine"), [raions](/wiki/Raions_of_Ukraine "Raions of Ukraine") and hromadas.[[3]](#cite_note-:1-3)

On 12 June 2020 the [Government of Ukraine](/wiki/Government_of_Ukraine "Government of Ukraine") approved the territories and administrative centers of the hromadas, which cover settlements in all regions of Ukraine except for [Crimea](/wiki/Crimea "Crimea").[[4]](#cite_note-4) A total of 1470 hromadas were approved.

On 12 August 2020 the Sokoliv hromada of the Cherkasy Oblast became a part of the Zhashkiv hromada.[[5]](#cite_note-5) Thus, there were 1469 hromadas.

## Administrative tasks and objectives

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Hromada&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Administrative tasks and objectives")]

|  |  |
| --- | --- |
| [icon](/wiki/File:Question_book-new.svg) | This section **does not [cite](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citing_sources "Wikipedia:Citing sources") any [sources](/wiki/Wikipedia:Verifiability "Wikipedia:Verifiability")**. Please help [improve this section](/wiki/Special:EditPage/Hromada "Special:EditPage/Hromada") by [adding citations to reliable sources](/wiki/Help:Referencing_for_beginners "Help:Referencing for beginners"). Unsourced material may be challenged and [removed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Verifiability#Burden_of_evidence "Wikipedia:Verifiability"). *(June 2022)* *([Learn how and when to remove this message](/wiki/Help:Maintenance_template_removal "Help:Maintenance template removal"))* |

Each hromada carries out two types of task: own and commissioned. Own tasks are public tasks exercised by self-government, which serve to satisfy the needs of the community. The tasks can be twofold:

* compulsory – where the municipality cannot decline to carry out the tasks, and must set up a budget to carry them out in order to provide the inhabitants with the basic public benefits.
* optional – where the municipality can carry them out in accordance with available budgetary means, set out only to specific local needs (on the hromada's own responsibility and budget).

### Own objectives

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Hromada&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Own objectives")]

Own high objectives include matters such as spatial harmony, real estate management, environmental protection and nature conservation, [water management](/wiki/Water_resource_management "Water resource management"), country roads, public streets, bridges, squares and traffic systems, water supply systems and source, the sewage system, removal of urban waste, water treatment, maintenance of cleanliness and order, sanitary facilities, dumps and council waste, supply of electric and thermal energy and gas, public transport, health care, welfare, care homes, subsidised housing, public education, cultural facilities including public libraries and other cultural institutions, historic monuments conservation and protection, the sports facilities and tourism including recreational grounds and devices, marketplaces and covered markets, green spaces and public parks, communal graveyards, public order and safety, fire and flood protection with equipment maintenance and storage, maintaining objects and devices of the public utility and administrative buildings, pro-family policy including social support for pregnant women, medical and legal care, supporting and popularising the self-government initiatives and cooperation within the commune including with non-governmental organizations, interaction with regional communities from other countries, etc.

### Commissioned tasks

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Hromada&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Commissioned tasks")]

Commissioned tasks cover the remaining public tasks resulting from legitimate needs of the state, commissioned by central government for the units of local government to implement. The tasks are handed over on the basis of statutory by-laws, charters and regulations, or by way of agreements between the self-government units and central-government administration.
